annotations.label: Probability space

anchor.snippet: is a mathematical construct that provides a formal model of a random process or experiment

annotations.note: A probability space is a measure space whose measure is a probability measure, captured by the `IsProbabilityMeasure` typeclass (`measure_univ = 1`).

annotations.label: Sample space

anchor.snippet: which is the set of all possible outcomes of a random process under consideration

annotations.note: The sample space is just the ambient type carrying a `MeasurableSpace`/`MeasureSpace`; Mathlib has no separately named 'sample space' declaration.

annotations.label: Event space

anchor.snippet: where an event is a subset of outcomes in the sample space

annotations.note: The event space (a σ-algebra) is exactly the `MeasurableSpace` structure, closed under complement and countable union.

annotations.label: Probability function

anchor.snippet: which assigns, to each event in the event space, a probability

annotations.note: The probability function is a `Measure` constrained by `IsProbabilityMeasure` to assign total mass one.

annotations.label: Throwing a die

anchor.snippet: one can define a probability space which models the throwing of a dice

annotations.note: This illustrative die example is not present in Mathlib, though uniform distributions on finite types exist generally.

annotations.label: Probability space triplet

anchor.snippet: that presents a model for a particular class of real-world situations

annotations.note: Mathlib models the (Ω, F, P) triple as a `MeasureSpace` plus an `IsProbabilityMeasure` typeclass rather than as one bundled structure.

annotations.label: Sample space and outcome

anchor.snippet: is the set of all possible outcomes

annotations.note: The sample space is the underlying type and an outcome an element of it; neither is a distinct named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: σ-algebra of events

anchor.snippet: is a collection of all the events we would like to consider

annotations.note: `MeasurableSpace` is the σ-algebra of events with fields `measurableSet_empty`, `measurableSet_compl`, `measurableSet_iUnion`.

annotations.label: Event

anchor.snippet: is a set of zero or more outcomes; that is, a subset of the sample space

annotations.note: An event (a measurable subset of the sample space) is `MeasurableSet s`.

annotations.label: Probability measure

anchor.snippet: is a set function returning an event's probability

annotations.note: `ProbabilityMeasure` is the bundled type of measures with total mass one (equivalently `Measure` with `IsProbabilityMeasure`).

annotations.label: Probability space (short)

anchor.snippet: a probability space is a measure space such that the measure of the whole space is equal to one

annotations.note: `IsProbabilityMeasure` is literally defined by the single field `measure_univ : μ univ = 1`.

annotations.label: Probability space (expanded)

anchor.snippet: The expanded definition is the following

annotations.note: `MeasureSpace` extends `MeasurableSpace` (the σ-algebra axioms) with a measure; adding `IsProbabilityMeasure` gives the expanded probability-space definition.

annotations.label: Closed under countable intersections

anchor.snippet: is also closed under countable intersections

annotations.note: `MeasurableSet.iInter` proves a countable intersection of measurable sets is measurable.

annotations.label: Discrete probability distribution

anchor.snippet: can be treated as events

annotations.note: `PMF α` (a function `α → ℝ≥0∞` summing to 1) is exactly a discrete probability distribution / probability mass function.

annotations.label: σ-algebra from partition

anchor.snippet: corresponds to a finite or countable partition

annotations.note: Mathlib has σ-algebra generation (`generateFrom`) but no specific lemma stating the bijection between countable partitions and discrete σ-algebras.

annotations.label: Empty probability case

anchor.snippet: is permitted by the definition, but rarely used

annotations.note: This is an informal remark about degenerate zero-probability outcomes, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Atoms

anchor.snippet: such ω are called atoms

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the negation `NoAtoms` (all singletons have measure zero) rather than a named definition of an atom of a measure.

annotations.label: Atomic/non-atomic decomposition

anchor.snippet: the probability space decomposes into a discrete (atomic) part

annotations.note: The decomposition of a measure into an atomic part plus a non-atomic part is not formalized in Mathlib (only the Lebesgue/Hahn-type decompositions exist).

annotations.label: Non-atomic implies uncountable

anchor.snippet: then Ω must be uncountable

annotations.note: `Set.Countable.measure_zero` shows countable sets are null under `NoAtoms`, which yields uncountability when total mass is one, but the conclusion is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Measurable sets

anchor.snippet: are called measurable

annotations.note: Sets belonging to the σ-algebra are exactly `MeasurableSet`.

annotations.label: Complete probability space

anchor.snippet: is said to be a complete probability space if for all

annotations.note: `Measure.IsComplete` (every null set is measurable) is the general completeness property; a complete probability space is the special case where the measure is a probability measure.

annotations.label: Single fair coin flip

anchor.snippet: If the experiment consists of just one flip of a fair coin

annotations.note: `PMF.bernoulli p` models a coin flip on `Bool`; a fair coin is the special case p = 1/2, but no fair-coin example is stated as such.

annotations.label: Coin tossed three times

anchor.snippet: The fair coin is tossed three times.

annotations.note: This particular three-toss worked example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sampling California voters

anchor.snippet: If 100 voters are to be drawn randomly from among all voters in California

annotations.note: This applied sampling example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform number on [0,1]

anchor.snippet: A number between 0 and 1 is chosen at random, uniformly.

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `volume` on the unit interval `I = [0,1]` is an `IsProbabilityMeasure` (an anonymous instance), formalizing the uniform distribution on [0,1].

annotations.label: Coin tossed endlessly

anchor.snippet: A fair coin is tossed endlessly.

annotations.note: An infinite Bernoulli sequence (product measure on {H,T}^ℕ) is not formalized as this specific example.

annotations.label: Random variable

anchor.snippet: A random variable X is a measurable function

annotations.note: A random variable is a measurable function, defined as `Measurable f` (preimages of measurable sets are measurable).

annotations.label: Pr(X ∈ A) notation

anchor.snippet: is a commonly used shorthand for

annotations.note: Pr(X ∈ A) = μ(X⁻¹ A) is the pushforward (law) `(μ.map X) A` via `Measure.map`, though the shorthand notation itself is not defined.

annotations.label: Power set for countable Ω

anchor.section: Defining the events in terms of the sample space
anchor.snippet: which is trivially a σ-algebra and the biggest one we can create using Ω

annotations.note: The `⊤` measurable space makes every set measurable (the power set) and is the top of the σ-algebra `CompleteLattice`.

annotations.label: Borel σ-algebra for uncountable Ω

anchor.section: Defining the events in terms of the sample space
anchor.snippet: which is the smallest σ-algebra that makes all open sets measurable

annotations.note: `borel α := generateFrom {s | IsOpen s}` is the smallest σ-algebra containing the open sets.

annotations.label: Conditional probability

anchor.snippet: Every set A with non-zero probability

annotations.note: `ProbabilityTheory.cond μ s` (notation `μ[|s]`) is the conditional probability measure given `s`.

anchors.snippet: Every set A with non-zero probability

annotations.label: Conditional probability is a measure

anchor.snippet: is itself a probability measure

annotations.note: `cond_isProbabilityMeasure` proves `μ[|s]` is an `IsProbabilityMeasure` when `μ s ≠ 0` (finite case).

annotations.label: Independent events

anchor.snippet: are said to be independent if P ( A ∩ B ) = P ( A ) P ( B )

annotations.note: `IndepSet s t μ` formalizes independence of two events, characterized by μ(s ∩ t) = μ(s)·μ(t).

annotations.label: Independent random variables

anchor.snippet: are said to be independent if any event defined in terms of X

annotations.note: `IndepFun f g μ` formalizes independence of two random variables via independence of their generated σ-algebras.

annotations.label: Mutually exclusive events

anchor.snippet: are said to be mutually exclusive or disjoint if

annotations.note: Mutual exclusivity (A ∩ B = ∅) is the order-theoretic `Disjoint A B` specialized to sets.

annotations.label: Additivity for disjoint events

anchor.snippet: If A and B are disjoint events, then

annotations.note: `measure_union` gives μ(s₁ ∪ s₂) = μ(s₁) + μ(s₂) for disjoint sets.

annotations.label: And/Or event notation

anchor.snippet: The event A ∩ B is referred to as

annotations.note: The 'A and B' / 'A or B' events are the set operations `Set.inter` (∩) and `Set.union` (∪); only the probabilistic naming is informal.
